text: The surgeon observes a markedly contracted and distorted gallbladder with Hartman's pouch tightly packed with gallstones. Dense adhesions are present inferiorly and medially, complicating the identification of the common bile duct. The gallbladder is opened laterally to Hartman's pouch, revealing numerous large gallstones, which are individually removed and placed into a plastic specimen retrieval bag.
The surgeon opens Hartman's pouch inferiorly, extracting additional gallstones and placing them into a plastic specimen retrieval bag. The gallbladder is then irrigated to remove debris.

On the posterior wall of the gallbladder bed, a mucosal line opening greater than five millimeters in diameter is observed, identified as a major bile duct from Segment V. A three-milliliter choledochoscope is inserted into the opening, confirming it as a major hepatic duct draining directly into the gallbladder, consistent with a cholecystohepatic duct.

The choledochoscope is withdrawn and blindly directed distally into Hartman's pouch. The previously placed biliary stent is identified at the ampulla of Vater. The scope is advanced through the ampulla, pushing stone fragments and debris into the duodenum, before being withdrawn.

The surgeon visually inspects Hartman's pouch, identifying Moritzi syndrome type IIb, characterized by a fistula involving more than fifty percent of the common bile duct diameter. The presence of a cholecystohepatic duct is also confirmed. The decision is made to perform a Roux-en-Y cholecystogeogenostomy for drainage.
The surgeon excises and removes the distal portion of the gallbladder, completing the extraction process.

The surgeon dissects lower midline adhesions to gain access for creating a Roux limb. A standard Roux-en-Y jejunostomy is performed, featuring a 40 cm biliopancreatic limb, a 60 cm Roux limb, and a 2.5 cm stapled side-to-side jejuno-jejunostomy. Sutures are used to close the common enterotomy.

The surgeon creates a window through the transverse mesocolon anterior to the duodenum, allowing the Roux limb to be passed cranially into the operative field. A posterior row of running 3-0 polydioxanone suture is placed, starting inferiorly and running from caudal to cranial.

The surgeon reaches the apex of the suture line and opens the bowel for anastomosis using a 3mm hook cautery. An anterior row of running 3-0 polydioxanone suture is placed, starting inferiorly, tied, and run from caudal to cranial.

The surgeon ties the two suture lines together at the apex, completing the anastomosis. The Roux limb is then secured to the gallbladder bed using a 3-0 polyglactin suture to prevent rotation and tension on the anastomosis when the patient is upright.
A 15 French fluted silastic drain is inserted into the operative field, marking the conclusion of the surgical procedure.
